perturbation.original_text: Each Party covenants that for a period of two (2) years following the date of this Agreement, such Party will not, directly or indirectly, solicit for employment or hire any employee of the other Party or any of the other Party's subsidiaries with whom such Party has had contact or who became known to such Party in connection with its consideration of the Possible Transaction; provided, however, that the foregoing provision will not prevent a Party from employing any employee of the other Party who contacts the prospective employer Party on his or her own initiative without any direct or indirect solicitation by or encouragement from the prospective employer Party, or prevent a general solicitation conducted in the ordinary course of business and not directed specifically at the employees of the other Party.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party covenants that for a period of ten (10) years following the date of this Agreement, such Party will not, directly or indirectly, solicit for employment or hire any employee of the other Party or any of the other Party's subsidiaries with whom such Party has had contact or who became known to such Party in connection with its consideration of the Possible Transaction; provided, however, that the foregoing provision will not prevent a Party from employing any employee of the other Party who contacts the prospective employer Party on his or her own initiative without any direct or indirect solicitation by or encouragement from the prospective employer Party, or prevent a general solicitation conducted in the ordinary course of business and not directed specifically at the employees of the other Party.
perturbation.explanation: Extended the non-solicitation period to 10 years. Non-compete agreements, including non-solicitation agreements, are generally unenforceable under California law if they extend for an unreasonable duration. While this agreement is governed by Florida law, a 10 year non-solicitation clause may be deemed unreasonably long in many jurisdictions.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600 states that "every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void." A 10-year non-solicitation agreement could be viewed as an unreasonable restraint on trade, conflicting with this law, especially if applied to California-based employees or operations.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)

perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the non-solicitation period to ten years, which contradicts California Business and Professions Code Section 16600, as explained in the Law Explanation. Both Scraped Law Snippet 1 and Scraped Law Snippet 2 state that "every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void." A ten-year non-solicitation agreement is likely viewed as an unreasonable restraint on trade, thus creating a legal contradiction with the law.

perturbation.original_text: All Parties' obligations under this Agreement will expire one year from the date of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: All Parties' obligations under this Agreement will expire ninety days from the date of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Reduced the confidentiality period to 90 days. This timeframe could be insufficient to protect trade secrets, conflicting with the reasonable measures required to maintain trade secret protection under the Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA).
perturbation.law_explanation: 18 U.S.C. § 1835 states that "trade secrets may be misappropriated if they are kept confidential using reasonable measures." A short, 90-day confidentiality period might be seen as failing to provide reasonable protection for trade secrets, potentially jeopardizing their legal status as such. While this doesn't directly violate the law, it makes trade secret protections much weaker, which can lead to a violation of DTSA.

Orders to preserve confidentiality (a) In General .-In any prosecution or other proceeding under this chapter, the court shall enter such orders and take such other action as may be necessary and appropriate to preserve the confidentiality of trade secrets, consistent with the requirements of the Federal Rules of Criminal and Civil Procedure, the Federal Rules of Evidence, and all other applicable laws. perturbation.justification: The original text specified a one-year confidentiality period, while the changed text reduces it to ninety days. The law explanation suggests this shorter period might not provide reasonable protection for trade secrets, potentially jeopardizing their legal status under 18 U.S.C. § 1835, which requires 'reasonable measures' to maintain confidentiality. Scraped Law Snippet 2 references 18 USC 1835: Orders to preserve confidentiality, reinforcing the relevance of this law. The reduction in the confidentiality period directly weakens the protection afforded to trade secrets, thus contradicting the requirement for reasonable measures.

perturbation.original_text: 5. SUBPOENAS, ETC. If the Recipient or any of its Representatives becomes required by law or applicable legal process (by deposition, interrogatory, request for documents, subpoena, civil investigative demand or similar process) to disclose any Evaluation Material furnished by the Provider, <PAGE> the Recipient will provide the Provider with prompt prior written notice of such requirement and the terms of and circumstances surrounding such requirement so that the Provider may seek an appropriate protective order or other remedy, or waive compliance with the terms of this Agreement, and the Recipient will provide such cooperation with respect to obtaining a protective order or other remedy as the Provider may reasonably request. If such protective order or other remedy is not obtained, or if the Provider is required to waive compliance with the provisions hereof, Recipient will furnish only that portion of such Evaluation Material which, as it is advised in a written opinion by its counsel, is legally required to furnish and will exercise all reasonable efforts to obtain an order or other reasonable assurance that confidential treatment, if available, will be accorded such Evaluation Material.
perturbation.changed_text: 5. SUBPOENAS, ETC. If the Recipient or any of its Representatives becomes required by law or applicable legal process (by deposition, interrogatory, request for documents, subpoena, civil investigative demand or similar process) to disclose any Evaluation Material furnished by the Provider, <PAGE> the Recipient will, within one business day of such request, furnish the Evaluation Material. The Recipient is not required to notify the Provider of the request for information.
perturbation.explanation: Eliminated the requirement to notify the Provider of a subpoena or legal request for confidential information and stipulated a one-day deadline for compliance. This contradicts standard legal practices and FRCP rules requiring reasonable notice and opportunity to object to subpoenas.
perturbation.law_explanation: FRCP 45(a)(4) requires that a party issuing a subpoena take reasonable steps to avoid imposing undue burden or expense on a person subject to the subpoena. Failing to provide notice to the Provider and demanding compliance within one business day could be deemed an undue burden and violate the intent of FRCP 45.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 45. Subpoena | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 45. Subpoena (a) In General. (1) Form and Contents. (A) Requirements—In General. Every subpoena must: (i) state the court from which it issued; (ii) state the title of the action and its civil-action number; (iii) command each person to whom it is directed to do the following at a specified time and place: attend and testify; produce designated documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things in that person's possession, custody, or control; or permit the inspection of premises; and (iv) set out the text of Rule 45(d) and (e). (B) Command to Attend a Deposition—Notice of the Recording Method. A subpoena commanding attendance at a deposition must state the method for recording the testimony. (C) Combining or Separating a Command to Produce or to Permit Inspection; Specifying the Form for Electronically Stored Information. A command to produce documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things or to permit the inspection of premises may be included in a subpoena commanding attendance at a deposition, hearing, or trial, or may be set out in a separate subpoena. A subpoena may specify the form or forms in which electronically stored information is to be produced. (D) Command to Produce; Included Obligations. A command in a subpoena to produce documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things requires the responding person to permit inspection, copying, testing, or sampling of the materials. (2) Issuing Court. A subpoena must issue from the court where the action is pending. (3) Issued by Whom. The clerk must issue a subpoena, signed but otherwise in blank, to a party who requests it. That party must complete it before service. An attorney also may issue and sign a subpoena if the attorney is authorized to practice in the issuing court. (4) Notice to Other Parties Before Service. If th

Secure .gov websites use HTTPS A lock ( Lock Locked padlock icon ) or https:// means you’ve safely connected to the .gov website. Share sensitive information only on official, secure websites. Federal Rules of Civil Procedure The Federal Rules of Civil Procedure govern civil proceedings in the United States district courts. The purpose of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure is "to secure the just, speedy, and inexpensive determination of every action and proceeding." Fed. R. Civ. P. 1. The rules were first adopted by order of the Supreme Court on December 20, 1937, transmitted to Congress on January 3, 1938, and effective September 16, 1938. The Civil Rules were last amended in 2024. perturbation.justification: The changed text eliminates the requirement for the Recipient to notify the Provider of a subpoena or legal request and mandates compliance within one business day. This contradicts the principle outlined in the Law Explanation, which states that FRCP 45(a)(4) requires parties issuing subpoenas to avoid imposing undue burden or expense. Scraped Law Snippet 1, which contains part of Rule 45, supports the existence of rules governing subpoenas. By eliminating notice and demanding rapid compliance, the changed text potentially imposes an undue burden on the Provider by removing their opportunity to seek a protective order, violating the intent of FRCP 45. The original text ensures provider has time to seek remedy, the changed text removes this right and creates inconsistency with legal best practice.

perturbation.original_text: 15. MISCELLANEOUS. This Agreement is for the benefit of the Parties and will be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Florida.
perturbation.changed_text: 15. MISCELLANEOUS. This Agreement is for the benefit of the Parties and will be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of California, without regard to its conflict of laws principles.
perturbation.explanation: Changed the governing law to California, without regard to conflict of laws. This may cause the contract to be interpreted differently if the parties are not headquartered in California. Furthermore, it changes the expectation for the parties if they were anticipating the rules and statutes to be interpreted in Florida. This could impact the enforceability of certain clauses, especially concerning non-competes.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: By changing the governing law to California, without regard to conflict of laws, the enforceability of the non-solicitation agreement in paragraph 8 is now subject to California Business and Professions Code Section 16600, which generally prohibits contracts that restrain anyone from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business. A 2 year restriction may not be enforceable. This change impacts the assumption the agreement would be construed under Florida law, which permits such agreements.

perturbation.justification: The changed text alters the governing law to California and disregards conflict of laws principles. According to California Business and Professions Code Section 16600, as stated in both scraped snippets, "every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void." This directly contradicts the enforceability of non-compete clauses, which may be permissible under Florida law but are generally prohibited in California, creating a legal contradiction as defined by the 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction' perturbation type.

perturbation.original_text: 12. EQUITABLE REMEDIES. The Parties acknowledge that money damages are an inadequate remedy for breach of this Agreement because of the difficulty of ascertaining the amount of damage that will be suffered in the event that this Agreement is breached. Therefore, each Party will be entitled to equitable relief, including injunction and specific performance, in the event of any breach of the provisions of this Agreement by the other Party or any of its Representatives, in addition to all other remedies available to such Party at law or in equity.
perturbation.changed_text: 12. EQUITABLE REMEDIES. The Parties agree that money damages are the sole and exclusive remedy for any breach of this Agreement. Neither party shall be entitled to equitable relief, including injunction and specific performance, regardless of the circumstances.
perturbation.explanation: Stipulated that monetary damages are the *only* available remedy, barring equitable relief. This contradicts the principle that trade secret misappropriation can be addressed through injunctions, as permitted under the Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA).
perturbation.contradicted_law: 18 U.S. Code § 1836 - Civil proceedings to prevent actual or threatened misappropriation
perturbation.law_explanation: 18 U.S.C. § 1836(b)(3)(A) specifically allows courts to grant injunctions to prevent actual or threatened misappropriation of trade secrets. By stating that money damages are the sole remedy and barring equitable relief, the modified text directly contradicts the remedies explicitly permitted under the DTSA.

Civil proceedings (a) The Attorney General may, in a civil action, obtain appropriate injunctive relief against any violation of this chapter. (b) Private Civil Actions.- (1) In general .-An owner of a trade secret that is misappropriated may bring a civil action under this subsection if the trade secret is related to a product or service used in, or intended for use in, interstate or foreign commerce. (2) Civil seizure.- (A) In
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text explicitly states that money damages are the 'sole and exclusive remedy' and bars equitable relief, including injunctions. This directly contradicts 18 U.S.C. § 1836, as shown in Scraped Law Snippet 1, which allows for injunctive relief in civil proceedings related to trade secret misappropriation. The original text allowed for equitable relief, while the changed text explicitly prohibits it, creating a direct conflict with the federal statute.
